text: The surgeon addresses adhesions of the omentum in the right lower quadrant, which are attached to the left pelvic brim and the cecum.
The surgeon elevates the sigmoid colon over the sacral promontory and initiates a medial to lateral dissection, carefully separating the colon from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon performs a quick visual check of the ureter before proceeding to isolate the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) for division.
The surgeon exposes the vessel, ensuring proximity to the takeoff of the left colic artery. Adventitial tissue is meticulously cleared from the vessel before division.
The surgeon initiates the lateral dissection of the sigmoid colon, joining it with the previously dissected medial plane over the sacral promontory.
The surgeon has inadvertently dissected deeper into the retroperitoneum and is currently adjusting the dissection to correct the depth.
The surgeon identifies the ureter within the crackly tissue and locates the surgical sponge placed nearby, ensuring careful navigation around these structures.
The surgeon demonstrates the total mesorectal excision (TME) technique, with the surgical field largely unedited except for brief camera cleaning.

The surgeon operates with the camera positioned in port three, utilizing two left arms and one right arm to maintain dissection within the adventitial plane. The hypogastric nerves are clearly visible within this plane.
The surgeon continues the dissection, working circumferentially around the tissue, adjusting tension and repositioning as needed, progressing down to the levator floor.
The surgeon identifies a tumor located approximately six centimeters within the mildly narrowed male pelvis.
The surgeon marks around the anterior aspect of the surgical field, preparing for further dissection or manipulation.
The surgeon attempts to enter the plane between Denonvilliers' fascia and the holy plane, navigating the anatomical layers during the colectomy procedure.
The surgeon performs lateral dissection, aided by the previously completed posterior dissection, with visible anatomical structures and tissue planes being carefully separated.

The surgeon continues to work circumferentially around the surgical site, carefully maneuvering to avoid collisions of the robotic arms. The dissection progresses anteriorly beneath the seminal vesicles.
The surgeon continues to work circumferentially around the mesorectum, carefully dissecting and separating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon palpates the area below the tumor to assess the position and determine the necessary extent for achieving an adequate distal margin.
The surgeon navigates beneath the prostate anteriorly, continuing the dissection in the pelvic region.
The surgeon encounters difficulty with arm four colliding with arm one while maneuvering to the left side. The vessel sealer is used for improved hemostatic control and efficient tissue division.
The surgeon follows the mesorectum along the pelvic floor, coning in towards the elevator floor, with visible tissue manipulation in the pelvic region.
The surgeon cleans the most distal rectum, removing the surrounding mesorectum tissue.
The surgeon uses a stapler from arm four to staple the very distal rectum, moving from anterior to posterior. The angle allows for effective stapling in this region.

The surgeon palpates below the interrectal ring with a finger to confirm the position, ensuring the stapler cannot be moved further distally. The stapler is then loaded for another pass across the tissue.

The surgeon examines the final dissection, with the specimen exteriorized and an anvil placed for the distal colorectal anastomosis. The preference is to use a stapler for the anastomosis due to limited visibility for sewing.

The surgeon connects the anvil to the distal colon and then operates the robotic system to release the intra-abdominal portion of the anvil. Subsequently, the surgeon returns to manually tighten the stapler for the anastomosis.
The specimen is exteriorized through arm three, the planned site for the ileostomy. The stapler is positioned in arm four, reflecting initial uncertainty about stoma creation.
